item.title: On view through August 9 at M+, “Lee Bul: 1998 to Now” is a comprehensive survey...
item.description:
@artforum

On view through August 9 at M+, “Lee Bul: 1998 to Now” is a comprehensive survey of the South Korean artist’s career. Featuring major works from Lee Bul’s studio and collections across Asia and beyond, the exhibition traces the pivotal developments and visual language of one of Asia’s more prominent contemporary artists. Plan your visit via @mplusmuseum.

Images:
“Lee Bul: From 1998 to Now,” 2026. Installation view, M+, Hong Kong, 2026. Photo: Lok Cheng.
“Lee Bul: From 1998 to Now,” 2026. Installation view, M+, Hong Kong, 2026. Photo: Dan Leung.
“Lee Bul: From 1998 to Now,” 2026. Installation view, M+, Hong Kong, 2026. Photo: Lok Cheng.
“Lee Bul: From 1998 to Now,” 2026. Installation view, M+, Hong Kong, 2026. Photo: Wilson Lam.
“Lee Bul: From 1998 to Now,” 2026. Installation view, M+, Hong Kong, 2026. Photo: Wilson Lam.
“Lee Bul: From 1998 to Now,” 2026. Installation view, M+, Hong Kong, 2026. Photo: Lok Cheng.
item.content:encoded:
@artforum

On view through August 9 at M+, “Lee Bul: 1998 to Now” is a comprehensive survey of the South Korean artist’s career. Featuring major works from Lee Bul’s studio and collections across Asia and beyond, the exhibition traces the pivotal developments and visual language of one of Asia’s more prominent contemporary artists. Plan your visit via @mplusmuseum.

Images:
“Lee Bul: From 1998 to Now,” 2026. Installation view, M+, Hong Kong, 2026. Photo: Lok Cheng.
“Lee Bul: From 1998 to Now,” 2026. Installation view, M+, Hong Kong, 2026. Photo: Dan Leung.
“Lee Bul: From 1998 to Now,” 2026. Installation view, M+, Hong Kong, 2026. Photo: Lok Cheng.
“Lee Bul: From 1998 to Now,” 2026. Installation view, M+, Hong Kong, 2026. Photo: Wilson Lam.

“Lee Bul: From 1998 to Now,” 2026. Installation view, M+, Hong Kong, 2026. Photo: Lok Cheng.
item.author: artforum

item.title: “Across his multimedia practice, Kamrooz Aram’s project is one of disrupting the...
item.description:
@artforum

“Across his multimedia practice, Kamrooz Aram’s project is one of disrupting the habits of classification that render aesthetic judgment perfunctory,” writes Julian Stern. An installation by the Iran-born, Brooklyn-based artist is currently on view in the 2026 Whitney Biennial. In Artforum’s April 2026 issue, Stern considers Aram’s “Variations on Glazed Bricks,” a 2021 series that investigates the kind of discourse “that Edward Said critiques for furnishing the West with authority over the East, an instrument of cultural and geopolitical domination.”

Link in bio.

Images:
Kamrooz Aram, Variations on Glazed Bricks, 2021, oil, colored pencil, and book pages on linen, five parts, each 20 × 16".
Kamrooz Aram, Descendants (Luster on Blue Glaze), 2025, oil, pencil, and book pages on linen, two parts, overall 24 × 40″.
Kamrooz Aram, Arabesque Composition (Archipelago), 2025, oil, oil crayon, and pencil on linen, hinged with artist’s frame, five parts, overall 7′ 5/8″ × 10′ 11 1/2 × 1 1/4″.
Bricks with palmette motif, Achaemenid period, Susa, Iran, ca. 6th–4th century BCE, ceramic, glaze, 7 1/2 × 5 1/2 × 3 3/8″.
@j.sterno @kamroozegar @whitneymuseum #WhitneyBiennial #ArtforumApril2026
item.content:encoded:
@artforum

“Across his multimedia practice, Kamrooz Aram’s project is one of disrupting the habits of classification that render aesthetic judgment perfunctory,” writes Julian Stern. An installation by the Iran-born, Brooklyn-based artist is currently on view in the 2026 Whitney Biennial. In Artforum’s April 2026 issue, Stern considers Aram’s “Variations on Glazed Bricks,” a 2021 series that investigates the kind of discourse “that Edward Said critiques for furnishing the West with authority over the East, an instrument of cultural and geopolitical domination.”

Link in bio.

Images:
Kamrooz Aram, Variations on Glazed Bricks, 2021, oil, colored pencil, and book pages on linen, five parts, each 20 × 16".
Kamrooz Aram, Descendants (Luster on Blue Glaze), 2025, oil, pencil, and book pages on linen, two parts, overall 24 × 40″.
Kamrooz Aram, Arabesque Composition (Archipelago), 2025, oil, oil crayon, and pencil on linen, hinged with artist’s frame, five parts, overall 7′ 5/8″ × 10′ 11 1/2 × 1 1/4″.
Bricks with palmette motif, Achaemenid period, Susa, Iran, ca. 6th–4th century BCE, ceramic, glaze, 7 1/2 × 5 1/2 × 3 3/8″.
@j.sterno @kamroozegar @whitneymuseum #WhitneyBiennial #ArtforumApril2026
item.author: artforum

item.title: A folded steel sculpture by Carol Bove from 2016 appears on the March 2026 cover...
item.description:
@artforum

A folded steel sculpture by Carol Bove from 2016 appears on the March 2026 cover of Artforum. “I know it’s wrong, clearly and demonstrably, to view this object as anything but hard steel, yet one sees it wrongly,” Hughes writes of a similar 2018 work, describing it “as simultaneously light and heavy, soft and hard, pliable and inflexible, inflating and deflating, pushing out and crushing in.” Bove embraces the “age-old sculptural ambition to defeat the physical properties” of her materials, placing her in a lineage with Bernini as well as a range of modernist sculptors; her works make “visible the theatricality that is the very hallmark of our time” by exposing “the underlying mechanism that drives theatricality: display.”

Also in the issue:
—Patrick R. Crowley traces the feedback loops of mimesis, from the ancients to AI
—Tschabalala Self shares her Top Ten
—Christopher Mead walks us through the architecture of Bruce Goff
—Anel Rakhimzhanova, Inga Lāce, and Joan Kee explore the range of contemporary art in Central Asia as new institutions and initiatives take shape in the region
—Joanna Mytkowska remembers the critic and curator Anka Ptaszkowska
+ more.

On the cover: Carol Bove, Hylomorph I, 2016, found steel, steel, urethane paint, 71 1⁄2 × 42 × 51". Photo: Dan Bradica.

#GordonHughes #CarolBove @guggenheim @marbleindex @tschabalalaself #ChristopherMead #BruceGoff @bul_anel @lace.inga #JoanKee #JoannaMytkowska #AnkaPtaszkowska #ArtforumMarch2026
item.content:encoded:
@artforum

A folded steel sculpture by Carol Bove from 2016 appears on the March 2026 cover of Artforum. “I know it’s wrong, clearly and demonstrably, to view this object as anything but hard steel, yet one sees it wrongly,” Hughes writes of a similar 2018 work, describing it “as simultaneously light and heavy, soft and hard, pliable and inflexible, inflating and deflating, pushing out and crushing in.” Bove embraces the “age-old sculptural ambition to defeat the physical properties” of her materials, placing her in a lineage with Bernini as well as a range of modernist sculptors; her works make “visible the theatricality that is the very hallmark of our time” by exposing “the underlying mechanism that drives theatricality: display.”

Also in the issue:
—Patrick R. Crowley traces the feedback loops of mimesis, from the ancients to AI
—Tschabalala Self shares her Top Ten
—Christopher Mead walks us through the architecture of Bruce Goff
—Anel Rakhimzhanova, Inga Lāce, and Joan Kee explore the range of contemporary art in Central Asia as new institutions and initiatives take shape in the region
—Joanna Mytkowska remembers the critic and curator Anka Ptaszkowska
+ more.

On the cover: Carol Bove, Hylomorph I, 2016, found steel, steel, urethane paint, 71 1⁄2 × 42 × 51". Photo: Dan Bradica.

#GordonHughes #CarolBove @guggenheim @marbleindex @tschabalalaself #ChristopherMead #BruceGoff @bul_anel @lace.inga #JoanKee #JoannaMytkowska #AnkaPtaszkowska #ArtforumMarch2026
item.author: artforum
